title: The Boring Money Trick That Beats Most Traders
style_anchor: simple hand-drawn stick figure cartoon on clean off-white paper, thick black marker outlines, minimal flat doodle style, lots of empty space, simple doodled props and labels, friendly whiteboard explainer aesthetic, 16:9
character_sheet_prompt: A simple friendly stick figure character drawn with thick black marker on off-white paper, round head, dot eyes, a small smile, the recurring main character of a whiteboard-style explainer about money and investing

shots.narration: The person who perfectly guesses the bottom of the market is usually poorer than the person who never guesses at all. Let me show you why.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure staring nervously at a jagged stock chart line on the wall, a big question mark doodled above its head, hand-drawn marker style

shots.narration: Meet our investor. Every month they get paid, and every month they face the same impossible question. Is now a good time to buy, or should I wait?
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure holding a small bag of money, frozen between two doodled arrows, one pointing to a buy button, one to a waiting clock

shots.narration: When prices go up, they feel afraid of overpaying. When prices fall, they feel afraid it will fall more. So they freeze, and do nothing.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure standing completely still and sweating, surrounded by chaotic up and down doodled arrows and panic lines

shots.narration: Psychologists call this analysis paralysis. Too many choices about timing, so the brain just refuses to act at all.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure with a tangled scribble drawn inside its round head, a doodled tangled knot representing a confused brain

shots.narration: Here is the trick that breaks the freeze. Instead of choosing when to buy, you choose a rule. Invest the same amount, on the same day, every single month.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure happily dropping the same coin into a doodled jar next to a wall calendar, a simple repeating arrow showing every month

shots.narration: This is called Dollar-Cost Averaging. Twenty-five dollars in January, twenty-five in February, no matter what the price is doing.
shots.image_prompt: A doodled calendar with a small twenty-five dollar coin drawn on each month, a stick figure giving a thumbs up beside it

shots.narration: And something quietly magical happens. When the price is high, your twenty-five dollars buys fewer shares. When the price crashes, that same twenty-five dollars buys a whole lot more.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure buying a few large doodled boxes when a price arrow is high, then buying many small boxes when the price arrow is low, split comparison drawing

shots.narration: So a market crash, the thing everyone fears, actually becomes a discount sale for the disciplined investor. Same money, more stuff.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure smiling and grabbing lots of doodled shopping items under a big hand-drawn sale tag, a falling chart line behind it

shots.narration: But now here is the twist that almost nobody tells you. Dollar-Cost Averaging usually does not make you the most money.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure looking shocked with wide dot eyes and a doodled exclamation mark, a chart behind it with a surprising twist in the line

shots.narration: A famous Vanguard study found that investing everything at once beat dollar-cost averaging about two-thirds of the time, ending with roughly two percent more wealth. Because markets mostly go up, money invested sooner simply grows longer.
shots.image_prompt: A doodled balance scale, one side labeled all at once tipping higher, the other labeled bit by bit, a stick figure scratching its head, hand-drawn chart trending up in the background

shots.narration: So why do it? Because the best returns on paper are worthless if you panic and sell at the bottom. Losses hurt about twice as much as gains feel good.
shots.image_prompt: A stick figure split into two doodles, one panicking and selling at a low point, one calmly holding, a big doodled broken heart over the panicking one

shots.narration: Dollar-Cost Averaging does not buy you the highest return. It buys you calm. It removes the one decision you would most regret, so you actually stay invested.
shots.image_prompt: A peaceful stick figure sleeping soundly in a doodled bed, a steady gently rising chart line drawn on the wall above, calm and content

shots.narration: And the funniest part? If you put money into a retirement fund from your paycheck, you are already doing it. You have been dollar-cost averaging this whole time. Just don't stop when the market turns red.
shots.image_prompt: A cheerful stick figure waving, standing next to a doodled piggy bank labeled retirement with a repeating monthly arrow, a small steady upward chart, friendly ending scene
